subject: mathematics
title: Probability
narrative: This rationalised NCERT chapter builds from conditional probability through the multiplication theorem, independence of events, the partition of a sample space and the theorem of total probability up to the capstone Bayes' theorem (reverse / a posteriori probability). The notion of a random variable is also introduced. Every node carries a clean verbatim source statement; the deterministic gates use the worked numeric examples of the chapter.

nodes.title: Compute a conditional probability
nodes.brief: Shrink the sample space to F, then measure how much of it is covered by E.
nodes.quote: the conditional probability of the event E given that F has occurred, i.e. P (EIF) is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition and formula.
P(E|F), the conditional probability of E given F, is defined as P(E intersect F) / P(F), provided P(F) is not 0. Knowing F has occurred re-scopes the sample space from S to F, so every probability is now measured relative to F.

PART 2 — Worked example: number problem.
Given P(A) = 7/13, P(B) = 9/13, P(A intersect B) = 4/13. Then P(A|B) = (4/13) / (9/13) = 4/9. Only the overlap 4/13 and the given event 9/13 matter; the individual P(A) is not used.

PART 3 — Worked example: die throw.
Throw a fair die. F = {even} = {2,4,6}, E = {>3} = {4,5,6}. E intersect F = {4,6}, so P(E intersect F) = 2/6 = 1/3 and P(F) = 3/6 = 1/2. Therefore P(E|F) = (1/3)/(1/2) = 2/3. Only outcomes in F are possible; two of those three outcomes also belong to E.
keyMoves: Identify the conditioning event F and check P(F) is not 0
Compute P(E intersect F) and P(F)
Divide: P(E|F) = P(E intersect F) / P(F)
misconceptions: Dividing by P(E) instead of P(F)

nodes.title: Use the complement property of conditional probability
nodes.brief: Once you know P(E|F), get P(E'|F) in one step by subtracting from 1.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why the property holds.
When we condition on F, the restricted sample space is F itself, so P(S|F) = 1. Since E and E' are disjoint with union S, it follows that P(E|F) + P(E'|F) = 1, giving P(E'|F) = 1 - P(E|F). The conditioning event F stays the same on both sides.

PART 2 — Worked example: direct subtraction.
If P(A|B) = 3/7, then P(A'|B) = 1 - 3/7 = 4/7. No new intersection calculation is needed; the complement rule does the work instantly.

PART 3 — Worked example: verify with original data.
If P(B|A) = 2/5, then P(B'|A) = 1 - 2/5 = 3/5. You can verify: P(B intersect A) + P(B' intersect A) must equal P(A), which is consistent since the two conditional probabilities sum to 1.
keyMoves: Compute or recall P(E|F)
Subtract from 1 to get P(E'|F)
Use the fact that P(S|F) = 1 to justify it
misconceptions: Forgetting the conditioning event F stays the same on both sides

nodes.title: Apply the multiplication rule of probability
nodes.brief: Rearrange the conditional probability formula to find P(E intersect F) from a product of two probabilities.
nodes.quote: The above result is known as the multiplication rule of probability.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The rule.
Rearranging P(E|F) = P(E intersect F)/P(F) gives P(E intersect F) = P(F) P(E|F) = P(E) P(F|E). Both forms are valid; choose whichever factor is easier to compute first. For three events: P(E intersect F intersect G) = P(E) P(F|E) P(G|EF).

PART 2 — Worked example: drawing without replacement.
A bag contains 10 black and 5 white balls. Draw two balls without replacement. P(both black) = P(1st black) x P(2nd black | 1st black) = (10/15) x (9/14) = 90/210 = 3/7. The denominator drops by 1 after the first draw because there is no replacement.

PART 3 — Worked example: card deck.
From a standard 52-card deck, draw 2 cards without replacement. P(both aces) = (4/52) x (3/51) = 12/2652 = 1/221. The second probability conditions on the first ace already being removed from the deck.
keyMoves: Identify the order in which the events occur
Multiply P(first) by the conditional probability of the next given the first
Extend the product for three or more events
misconceptions: Multiplying unconditional probabilities when the events are dependent
Forgetting to update the conditional probability after the first event (e.g. without replacement)

nodes.title: Test two events for independence
nodes.brief: Two events are independent when knowing one occurred does not change the other's probability.
nodes.quote: Let E and F be two events associated with the same random experiment, then E and F are said to be independent if
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
E and F are independent if P(E intersect F) = P(E) P(F). Equivalently, P(E|F) = P(E): knowing F occurred gives no new information about E. Independence is not the same as mutual exclusivity; two mutually exclusive events with non-zero probabilities are actually dependent.

PART 2 — Worked example: coin and die.
Toss a fair coin and roll a fair die. Let E = {heads}, F = {3 on die}. P(E intersect F) = 1/12, and P(E) P(F) = (1/2)(1/6) = 1/12. Since they are equal, E and F are independent. The outcome of the coin does not affect the die result.

PART 3 — Worked example: given probabilities.
P(A) = 1/3, P(B) = 1/4, P(A intersect B) = 1/12. Check: P(A) P(B) = (1/3)(1/4) = 1/12 = P(A intersect B). So A and B are independent.
keyMoves: Compute P(E), P(F) and P(E intersect F)
Check whether P(E intersect F) = P(E) P(F)
Conclude independent if equal, dependent if not
misconceptions: Confusing independent events with mutually exclusive events
Concluding independence from P(E|F) alone without checking the product rule

nodes.title: Test three events for mutual independence
nodes.brief: Three events need four separate product conditions — three pairwise and one triple — to be mutually independent.
nodes.quote: Three events A, B and C are said to be mutually independent, if
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Four conditions required.
A, B, C are mutually independent if all of the following hold: P(A intersect B) = P(A)P(B), P(A intersect C) = P(A)P(C), P(B intersect C) = P(B)P(C), and P(A intersect B intersect C) = P(A)P(B)P(C). Pairwise independence alone does not imply mutual independence; the triple-product condition is additional and necessary.

PART 2 — Worked example: three coin tosses.
Toss a fair coin three times. A = {H on 1st}, B = {H on 2nd}, C = {H on 3rd}. Each has probability 1/2. P(A intersect B) = 1/4 = (1/2)(1/2), and similarly for AC and BC. P(A intersect B intersect C) = 1/8 = (1/2)(1/2)(1/2). All four conditions hold, so A, B, C are mutually independent.

PART 3 — Worked example: triple product.
If A, B, C are mutually independent with P(A) = 1/2, P(B) = 1/3, P(C) = 1/4, then P(A intersect B intersect C) = (1/2)(1/3)(1/4) = 1/24. This only follows because mutual independence (all four conditions) has been established.
keyMoves: Verify each of the three pairwise product conditions
Verify the triple-product condition P(A and B and C) = P(A)P(B)P(C)
Declare mutual independence only if all four hold
misconceptions: Concluding mutual independence from pairwise independence alone

nodes.title: Recognise a partition of the sample space
nodes.brief: A partition slices S into non-overlapping, exhaustive pieces each with positive probability.
nodes.quote: the events  $E_1, E_2, ..., E_n$  represent a partition of the sample space S if they are pairwise disjoint, exhaustive and have nonzero probabilities.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Three conditions.
Events E1, E2, ..., En form a partition of S if: (i) they are pairwise disjoint — Ei intersect Ej = empty for all i not equal to j; (ii) they are exhaustive — E1 union E2 union ... union En = S; (iii) each has positive probability P(Ei) > 0. Any event E and its complement E' satisfy all three, making {E, E'} the simplest partition.

PART 2 — Worked example: die outcomes.
For a fair die S = {1,2,3,4,5,6}. Let E1 = {1,2}, E2 = {3,4}, E3 = {5,6}. These are pairwise disjoint, their union is S, and each has probability 1/3 > 0. So {E1, E2, E3} is a partition of S.

PART 3 — Common failure: overlap or gap.
The collection {{1,2,3}, {3,4,5,6}} fails because 3 appears in both sets (not disjoint). The collection {{1,2}, {3,4}} fails because outcomes 5 and 6 are not covered (not exhaustive). The collection {{1,2,3,4,5,6}, {}} fails because the empty set has probability 0 (condition iii). All three conditions must hold simultaneously.
keyMoves: Check the events are pairwise disjoint
Check their union is the whole sample space S
Check each has non-zero probability
misconceptions: Allowing overlapping events in a partition
Forgetting the events must cover the entire sample space

nodes.title: Apply the theorem of total probability
nodes.brief: Break P(A) into weighted conditional probabilities over every piece of a partition.
nodes.quote: Let A be any event associated with S, then
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The theorem.
If {E1, ..., En} is a partition of S with P(Ej) > 0 for each j, and A is any event, then P(A) = P(E1)P(A|E1) + P(E2)P(A|E2) + ... + P(En)P(A|En). Each term P(Ej)P(A|Ej) is the probability of A occurring via Ej; summing across all pieces of the partition gives the total probability of A.

PART 2 — Worked example: two bags.
Bag I has 3 red and 4 blue balls; Bag II has 2 red and 6 blue balls. A bag is selected at random (probability 1/2 each) and one ball is drawn. P(red) = (1/2)(3/7) + (1/2)(2/8) = 3/14 + 1/8 = 12/56 + 7/56 = 19/56.

PART 3 — Worked example: two machines.
Machine M1 produces 60% of items with 3% defect rate; M2 produces 40% with 2% defect rate. P(defective) = 0.60 x 0.03 + 0.40 x 0.02 = 0.018 + 0.008 = 0.026. The partition is {item from M1, item from M2}.
keyMoves: Identify a partition E1..En of the sample space
Find P(Ej) and the conditional P(A|Ej) for each part
Sum the products P(Ej)P(A|Ej) to get P(A)
misconceptions: Forgetting one part of the partition (sum no longer equals P(A))
Using P(Ej|A) instead of P(A|Ej) in the sum

nodes.title: Define a random variable
nodes.brief: A random variable assigns a single real number to each outcome of a random experiment.
nodes.quote: A random variable is a real valued function whose domain is the sample space of a random experiment.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition.
A random variable is a real-valued function whose domain is the sample space S of a random experiment. It assigns a real number to each outcome. The set of all values it can take is called its range.

PART 2 — Worked example: two coin tosses.
Toss a fair coin twice. S = {HH, HT, TH, TT}. Define X = number of heads. Then X(HH) = 2, X(HT) = 1, X(TH) = 1, X(TT) = 0. The range of X is {0, 1, 2}. Each outcome maps to exactly one number.

PART 3 — Worked example: computing P(X = 1).
Using the same X, P(X = 1) = P({HT, TH}) = 2/4 = 1/2. Multiple outcomes can share the same value of X; we add their probabilities. A different random variable Y = number of tails on the same sample space would give Y(HH)=0, Y(HT)=1, Y(TH)=1, Y(TT)=2 — showing two random variables can live on the same S.
keyMoves: Take the sample space of the experiment as the domain
Assign a real number to each outcome by a rule
Recognise this rule as a random variable
misconceptions: Thinking a random variable is itself a probability (it is a function on outcomes)
Believing only one random variable can be defined on a sample space

nodes.title: Apply Bayes' theorem to find a reverse probability
nodes.brief: Bayes' theorem inverts a conditional probability: from likelihoods P(A|Ei) to causes P(Ei|A).
nodes.quote: Bayes' theorem is also called the formula for the probability of
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The formula.
Given a partition E1, ..., En of S with P(Ej) > 0, and an event A with P(A) > 0, Bayes' theorem states: P(Ei|A) = P(Ei)P(A|Ei) / [sum over j of P(Ej)P(A|Ej)]. The P(Ei) are called prior probabilities and P(Ei|A) the posterior (a posteriori) probabilities. The denominator is exactly P(A) from the total probability theorem.

PART 2 — Worked example: two urns.
Urn I has 3 white and 2 black balls; Urn II has 1 white and 3 black balls. One urn is selected at random and one ball is drawn — it is white. P(Urn I | white) = [(1/2)(3/5)] / [(1/2)(3/5) + (1/2)(1/4)] = (3/10) / (3/10 + 1/8) = (3/10) / (17/40) = (3/10)(40/17) = 12/17.

PART 3 — Worked example: three bags.
Bags I, II, III have 2W1B, 3W2B, 4W3B. A bag is chosen at random (prior 1/3 each); a white ball is drawn. P(W|I) = 2/3, P(W|II) = 3/5, P(W|III) = 4/7. Denominator = (1/3)[2/3 + 3/5 + 4/7] = (1/3)(70/105 + 63/105 + 60/105) = (1/3)(193/105) = 193/315. P(Bag I | white) = (1/3)(2/3) / (193/315) = (2/9)(315/193) = 70/193.
keyMoves: Set up the partition (hypotheses) and their prior probabilities P(Ei)
Find the likelihoods P(A|Ei) and form the denominator by total probability
Apply P(Ei|A) = P(Ei)P(A|Ei) / sum P(Ej)P(A|Ej)
misconceptions: Confusing the prior P(Ei) with the posterior P(Ei|A)
Swapping P(A|Ei) and P(Ei|A) in the formula
